## Program (program-section)

- Program Guide
- Overview | Program Details | Full Schedule
- May — Family Month: An Invitation to a Royal Banquet Where Filial Love Blooms
- 'Changgyeonggung Yayeon' is an immersive performance experience that reimagines the royal banquet once offered by Crown Prince Hyomyeong to his father, King Sunjo, in the 19th century — a heartfelt tribute to a parent's love, now brought to life for families today. / Step into the roles of Joseon court officials and noble ladies, and give your parents a truly unforgettable gift: a night inside a royal palace.
- Event Dates
- Thu, May 7 – Sun, May 17, 2026
- Event Hours
- (Main Performance: 7:10 PM – 8:00 PM)
- Venue
- Munjeongjeon Hall & Surrounding Grounds, Changgyeonggung Palace
- Capacity
- 30 families per day
- (1 experience participant + up to 2 accompanying family members)
- Royal Court Official Experience — Receive an official court title — Minister, Senior Official, and more — dress in authentic court robes, and take your place at the king's banquet as a true member of the Joseon court. Father | Traditional court robe (danryeong) and official hat (samo), with full styling. Mother | Traditional layered ensemble (dangui, skirt, binyeo hairpin, cheobji ornament), with full styling.
- Royal Confections Experience — Savor an exquisite selection of traditional byeonggwa (Korean confections) once enjoyed by the Joseon royal family on their most celebrated occasions. A curated tasting of green tea yaksik, persimmon rolls, candied walnuts, and more.
- Royal Banquet & Performance — Enjoy an elegant spread of royal confections and tea while watching performances of classical court dance and traditional folk dance. / Bak Jeop Mu, Ye Do Mu, Seoljanggo Dance. *A mobile subtitle service is available to help you follow along with court ceremonies and performances that may be unfamiliar.
- Professional Photography & Videography — Longevity Portrait: A solo portrait of your parents in full traditional attire, captured by a professional photographer. Video Letter: A candid interview-style video in which parents and children share heartfelt messages with one another. Family Photo: A group portrait taken on stage after the performance, with the royal throne and ceremonial table as the backdrop. Candid Snapshots: Natural family moments captured throughout the Hyojabee filial piety experience.
- Filial Piety Experiences — Hyojabee: A playful family event — a Korean spin on the traditional first-birthday ritual (doljabee), this time with the parents as the star of the show. Filial Letter (Hyosim Pyeonji): Write a letter to your parents from the heart — to be delivered 6 months or 1 year from today. Royal Letter Hunt: Search the grounds of Changgyeonggung for hidden letters written by Crown Prince Hyomyeong — a treasure hunt with a story.
- Full Schedule 4:00 PM – 5:00 PM

## Performance (performance-section)

- Traditional Royal Court Performances
- Immerse yourself in the refined world of Joseon court dance and discover the depth and elegance of Korea's royal cultural heritage.
- Bak Jeop Mu (Butterfly Dance)
- An elegant dance depicting butterflies fluttering gracefully among the flowers
- Bak Jeop Mu is one of the court dances composed by Crown Prince Hyomyeong in the late Joseon period. It portrays pairs of colorful butterflies drifting on the spring breeze, brushing lightly against blossoms in the warm sunlight. Six dancers perform in flowing green costumes (noknrapo) adorned with embroidered butterflies, moving with grace and fluid beauty.
- Running Time
- 5 minutes
- Cast
- 6 performers, Korea Heritage Agency Performing Arts Ensemble
- Ye Do Mu (Dance of Devotion)
- A harmonious pairing of two court dances created by Crown Prince Hyomyeong — one for his father, one for his mother
- This performance brings together Musanhyang and Chunaengjeon — two dances composed by Crown Prince Hyomyeong of the Joseon Dynasty, one as a tribute to his father King Sunjo, and the other as an offering to his mother Queen Sunwon Sukwang. Together, they embody a son's profound devotion to both parents.
- Cast
- 3 performers, Korea Heritage Agency Performing Arts Ensemble
- Sub-programs
- Musanhyang — A dancer dressed in deep green stands atop a lacquered tray and performs with bold, sweeping movements, conveying a sense of dignity, grandeur, and powerful presence.
- Chunaengjeon — A dancer dressed in soft yellow stands on a flower-patterned mat and performs with gentle, refined movements, capturing the delicate poise of an oriole in spring.
- Seoljanggo Dance (Hourglass Drum Dance)
- A dynamic and spirited dance performed with a traditional hourglass drum worn across the shoulder
- The janggo drum is worn diagonally across the shoulder and secured at the waist, allowing the performer to play intricate rhythms while dancing simultaneously. Known as Seoljanggo or Seoljanggo Nori, this performance balances vibrant percussion with expressive movement, bringing traditional Korean rhythm and dance to life in an exhilarating display.
- Running Time
- 6 minutes
- [attribute] Bak Jeop Mu
- [attribute] Ye Do Mu
- [attribute] Seoljanggo Dance

## Royal Refreshments (food-section)

- Royal Confections
- Savor a curated selection of traditional Korean confections (byeonggwa) once enjoyed in the royal courts of the Joseon Dynasty.
- For Court Officials (Experience Participants) | For Accompanying Family Members
- Jeon-yak (Royal Herbal Jelly)
- Among the many prized delicacies of the Joseon royal household, jeon-yak was considered one of the most distinguished. This richly spiced herbal jelly was prepared with care and offered on special royal occasions.
- Key Ingredients
- Honey, gelatin (beef trotters, water), jujube, dried ginger powder, cinnamon powder, bellflower root, black pepper, cloves, sesame oil
- Dasik (Pressed Tea Confection)
- A traditional Korean confection made by kneading finely ground starch, pine pollen, or black sesame powder with honey or jocheong (malt syrup) and pressing the mixture into decorative molds. The molds are carved with auspicious symbols — wishes for longevity, health, and happiness — making dasik as meaningful as it is delicious.
- Key Ingredients
- Soybean (large), pumpkin seeds, honey
- Walnut Jeonggwa (Candied Walnuts)
- Walnuts are blanched three times in hot water, then slowly simmered in sugar and corn syrup before being lightly fried to a golden finish. Rich in unsaturated fatty acids, walnuts are celebrated for supporting brain health and skin vitality, making this confection as nourishing as it is indulgent.
- Key Ingredients
- Walnuts (imported), sugar, corn syrup, cooking oil
- Bellflower Root Jeonggwa (Candied Bellflower)
- Bellflower root (doraji) is blanched, then simmered with corn syrup or honey to create a subtly bittersweet confection with a distinctive aroma. Rich in saponin, doraji has long been used in Korean traditional medicine to support respiratory health, and is known to help soothe coughs, clear phlegm, and ease asthma symptoms.
- Key Ingredients
- Bellflower root, corn syrup, salt, soybean powder
- Gotgam Danji (Stuffed Persimmon Roll)
- Records show that persimmons have been cultivated in Korea since the Goryeo Dynasty, and they remained a royal favorite throughout the Joseon period — even appearing in official gift lists sent to China during the reign of King Sukjong. Gotgam Danji is made by stuffing dried persimmons with a fragrant filling of yujajeong (citron preserve), candied walnuts, and cinnamon, creating a confection of exceptional depth and flavor.
- Key Ingredients
- Dried persimmon, citron preserve, walnut, cinnamon powder, jujube
- Green Tea Yaksik (Sweet Steamed Rice Cake with Green Tea)
- Yaksik — also known as yakbap — is a beloved Korean sweet rice dish that has graced festive tables for centuries. Its origins trace back to a tale in the Samguk Yusa (Memoranda of the Three Kingdoms), where it was prepared as an offering of gratitude to a crow — a tradition that lives on in the Daeboreum (Great Full Moon) celebrations. The character yak (藥) in its name means "medicine," signifying that this dish is both delicious and deeply beneficial. Glutinous rice is soaked overnight in a savory-sweet soy and sugar brine, then steamed with chestnuts and jujubes, folded in sesame oil, and finished with a scattering of pine nuts.
- Key Ingredients
- Glutinous rice, salt, chestnuts, jujube, pine nuts, sugar, cinnamon powder, green tea powder, honey, sesame oil
- Lotus Root Danggwa (Crispy Lotus Root Chips)
- A winter-season delicacy with a story rooted in history — when the great Joseon scholar Yulgok (Yi I) fell gravely ill with grief after losing his beloved mother, the artist and poet Shin Saimdang, it was said that lotus root porridge helped restore his health. Lotus root is prized in traditional Korean medicine for its ability to stop bleeding, reduce inflammation, dissolve blood clots, and ease respiratory ailments. Here, it is thinly sliced, briefly blanched, soaked in salted water, dried, and fried to a satisfying crisp.
- Key Ingredients
- Lotus root, cooking oil, sugar, salt
- Gokcha (Roasted Grain Tea)
- A traditional Korean grain tea — warm, earthy, and deeply comforting. Barley, dried corn, and black soybeans are each dry-roasted separately to draw out their natural aromas, then simmered together in water and strained to create a smooth, subtly nutty brew that has been enjoyed in Korean households for generations.
- Key Ingredients
- Barley, corn, black soybean, Solomon's seal (Polygonatum)
- Pear Jeonggwa (Pear Preserve)
- Juicy Korean pear — packed with natural moisture and dietary fiber — is slowly simmered in honey or a light sugar syrup to create a gentle, floral preserve. Cherished for its benefits to digestion and respiratory health, this confection reflects the Joseon court's tradition of finding nourishment and beauty in the simplest of seasonal ingredients.
- Key Ingredients
- Pear, sugar, pine nuts
- Gaeseong Juak (Royal Fried Rice Cake)
- A prized delicacy of Joseon royal banquets, juak was once reserved for distinguished guests and the grandest of occasions. Glutinous rice flour and wheat flour are kneaded together with makgeolli (Korean rice wine) to create a soft, lightly fermented dough, which is then shaped and fried in oil until golden. Sweet, chewy, and irresistibly fragrant.
- Key Ingredients
- Glutinous rice flour, wheat flour, makgeolli (Korean rice wine), sugar, corn syrup, ginger, cooking oil
- A Note on the Refreshments
- All confections are prepared fresh on the day of the event, following traditional recipes with care.
- Please inform us in advance if you have any food allergies or dietary restrictions.
- The menu selection may be subject to minor changes depending on ingredient availability and freshness.

## Photo Spots (photospot-section)

- Photo Hotspots
- Discover the perfect backdrops for capturing beautiful memories amid the timeless beauty of Changgyeonggung.
- In Front of Myeongjeongjeon Hall
- The oldest surviving main throne hall of any Korean palace — where history and beauty stand side by side
- Highlights
- A genuinely historic backdrop with stunning visual depth
- A vast courtyard offering creative angles and wide compositions
- A more relaxed and uncrowded atmosphere compared to other royal palaces such as Gyeongbokgung
- Breathtaking scenery as the evening glow settles over the palace
- Best Time to Visit
- 5:30 PM – 6:20 PM (when the sunset light falls softly across the courtyard)
- Photography Tips
- Step back far enough to capture the full grandeur of Myeongjeongjeon in a single frame.
- The wide-open courtyard offers a rare opportunity — try exploring unconventional angles and distances.
- Okcheon Bridge
- The only palace bridge in Korea designated as a national treasure (Treasure No. 386) — and it's easy to see why
- Highlights
- A beautifully proportioned arched stone bridge with graceful, sweeping curves
- The only palace bridge with a natural stream of clear water flowing beneath it, creating a uniquely serene and poetic atmosphere
- A perfect sight line from the bridge — Myeongjeongmun Gate and Myeongjeongjeon Hall framed in a single view
- At its most magical during spring cherry blossom season and autumn foliage
- Best Time to Visit
- 3:00 PM – 5:00 PM (when the light softens to a warm, gentle glow)
- Photography Tips
- Stand to the side of the bridge and photograph your family from a comfortable distance — the arch and flowing water make for a beautifully natural frame.
- From directly in front of the bridge, frame your shot toward Myeongjeongmun Gate for a perfectly composed, layered composition.
- Photography Tips
- Expert guidance for capturing stunning photos at Changgyeonggung
- Hanbok Photography Tips
- Let the skirt catch a gentle breeze — even a light wind adds beautiful volume and movement to the chima.
- Find a pose that highlights the soft, curved lines of the jeogori jacket for an elegant silhouette.
- Traditional props like folding fans or norigae (ornamental pendants) add authentic charm and visual interest to your shots.
- Choose your shooting location with an eye for color harmony — let the palette of your hanbok complement the surroundings.
- Working with Light
- Shoot during the golden hour — one to two hours before sunset — for warm, flattering light that brings out the richness of hanbok colors.
- Use the natural shadows cast by palace buildings to add gentle depth and dimension to your portraits.
- Experiment with backlighting for a dreamy silhouette effect — especially beautiful against the sky.
- On overcast days, the soft, even light acts like a natural softbox, ideal for smooth, shadow-free portraits.
- Composition & Angle
- Use the lines of palace walls, gates, and rooftops as natural framing elements to draw the eye into your shot.
- Shooting from a low angle adds a sense of grandeur and drama — making your subjects and the architecture appear even more impressive.
- Apply the rule of thirds to create well-balanced, visually engaging compositions.
- Look for clean, uncluttered backgrounds to keep your subjects at the center of attention.

## Script-Generated Text

- en
- Welcome to the Royal Banquet of Changgyeonggung Palace.
- Tonight, we invite you to step into the living traditions of the Joseon Dynasty.
- We begin with the Bak Jeop Mu — the Butterfly Dance.
- As the soft fragrance of incense drifts gently through the air...
- Please sit back and let the graceful movements of our dancers carry you away.
- Next, we present Musanhyang from the Ye Do Mu — Dance of Devotion.
- A breathtaking work that captures the essence of mountain mist through the language of dance.
- Thank you for joining us for this evening's performance.
